A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Anselm of Canterbury

1033 – 1109

Faith in search of understanding: the mind’s ascent to God through reason alone.

St. Anselm does not write a modern treatise called “On the Mind.” But a remarkably coherent philosophy of mind can be extracted from the works where he treats divine wisdom, the rational soul, truth, free choice, angelic fall, grace, and eternal life. The relevant corpus is especially the Monologion, Proslogion, De Veritate, De Libertate Arbitrii, De Casu Diaboli, and De Concordia. Those works belong to a single intellectual project: Anselm’s “faith seeking understanding,” which is not a retreat from reason but an attempt to understand, by rigorous argument, what faith loves and seeks. Modern summaries rightly note that Anselm’s philosophical range extends well beyond the ontological argument, especially to divine nature, free choice, truth, justice, evil, grace, and the virtues.

The central thesis of this report is that Anselm’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a philosophy of rectified rational agency. For Anselm, mind is not merely an inner arena of representations. A mind is a rational, memory-bearing, self-reflexive, truth-answerable, love-capable power whose proper end is to know, remember, and love the supreme Good. Human mind, angelic mind, and divine mind are not three unrelated topics. They form a hierarchy: God is uncreated Mind, Wisdom, Truth, and Will in perfect simplicity; angels are finite rational minds without bodily embodiment; human souls are finite rational minds embodied, temporal, and wounded, but still ordered toward truth, justice, and divine love.

1. Method: why Anselm’s theology is philosophically relevant

Anselm’s famous formula, often paraphrased as “faith seeking understanding,” can mislead if taken to mean that he simply presupposes theological claims and then decorates them with argument. The point is subtler. For Anselm, faith is not merely belief-that; it is also a volitional and affective orientation, a love that seeks to understand what it loves. The Proslogion opens with the human being as a creature made in God’s image precisely in order to be mindful of God, to conceive God, and to love God. Anselm then prays that he may not merely believe but understand.

That matters for philosophy of mind because Anselm never treats cognition as separable from love, will, memory, and moral orientation. A mind is not healthy merely because it can form concepts; it is healthy when it is directed rightly toward truth, goodness, justice, and God. Likewise, the mind of God and the minds of angels are not detachable “theological” ornaments. They are limit cases in Anselm’s account of mentality. God shows what intellect, will, truth, love, and justice are when they are not fragmented, temporal, passive, or dependent. Angels show what finite rational agency looks like without the complications of bodily sensation and ordinary human weakness.

This means that the proper modern extraction is not “Anselm’s theory of consciousness” in a narrow post-Cartesian sense. It is an integrated account of rational soul, intentionality, self-knowledge, normativity, will, freedom, affective motivation, moral disorder, grace, and divine cognition.

2. Basic vocabulary: anima, mens, intellectus, memoria, voluntas

Anselm’s vocabulary is not identical to contemporary usage. Anima is the soul, especially the rational soul when discussing human beings. Mens is mind, often the rational or intellectual dimension of the soul. Intellectus and intelligentia concern understanding or intellectual grasp. Ratio is reason, the power to distinguish, judge, and order. Memoria is memory, but in Anselm’s Augustinian inheritance it is not merely episodic recollection; it is also the mind’s abiding possession of itself and of what can be called forth into thought. Voluntas means will, but in Anselm it can name the power of willing, an inclination or affection, or an actual act of willing.

Several normative terms are indispensable. Veritas is truth; rectitudo is rightness, correctness, or uprightness; iustitia is justice, especially uprightness of will preserved for its own sake. Anselm’s philosophy of mind cannot be reconstructed if “truth” is reduced to correspondence alone or “freedom” to unconstrained choice. His mature theory repeatedly asks whether speech, thought, will, and action fulfill what they ought to fulfill. In De Veritate, truth is treated as a kind of rightness perceptible by mind alone; in De Libertate Arbitrii, free choice is defined by its capacity to preserve uprightness of will for the sake of that uprightness.

A compact conceptual map would look like this:

Domain Anselmian concept Philosophical function
Cognitive understanding, conception, internal word the mind’s capacity to grasp, represent, and judge
Reflexive memory of self, thought of self the mind’s self-relation and interiority
Normative truth, rightness, justice the standard to which mental and volitional acts answer
Volitional will as power, inclination, and act the mind’s practical orientation toward goods
Affective love, desire for happiness, love of justice the motivational structure of rational agency
Moral free choice, uprightness, sin, privation the success or failure of rational agency
Theological divine Wisdom, Word, Love; angelic rationality the metaphysical limits and exemplars of mind

This already suggests why Anselm is closer to Augustine than to Descartes. The mind’s self-awareness matters, but self-awareness is not the final foundation. It is a mirror by which the rational creature may ascend toward God.

3. The created rational soul: mind as image, mirror, and end-directed power

Anselm’s most important statement about the human mind is that it is made in the image of God so that it may remember, understand, and love God. In the Proslogion, the human speaker laments that the image has been darkened by vice, but the basic structure remains: the human being was created to be mindful of God, to conceive God, and to love God.

The Monologion develops this into a theory of rational mind as the closest creaturely image of the supreme Being. The rational mind can remember, understand, and love itself; because of that triadic structure, it is a mirror of the divine Trinity. Anselm explicitly says that through the rational mind one makes the nearest approach to knowledge of the supreme Being, and that knowing one’s own nature helps raise the mind toward knowledge of God.

This is not merely a theological metaphor. It is an ontology of mind. A rational mind is not defined first by sensation, computation, or behavioral output. It is defined by a triadic reflexivity: memory, intelligence, and love. Memory gives the mind an abiding possession of itself and its objects; intelligence gives it conceptual and judgmental grasp; love gives it orientation toward value. A mind that remembers and understands but does not love rightly remains incomplete. Anselm says that rationality would be useless unless what the mind distinguishes as good, bad, true, false, just, unjust, advantageous, or disadvantageous were then loved or rejected accordingly.

This yields a strong claim: for Anselm, rationality is intrinsically practical and affective. To be rational is not simply to classify propositions. It is to discern in order to love, reject, choose, preserve, and act.

4. The mind’s intentional structure: being “in the understanding”

The Proslogion is usually read as a work of natural theology, but it also contains one of Anselm’s most philosophically interesting analyses of intentionality. In the famous discussion of “that than which nothing greater can be thought,” Anselm distinguishes between something existing in the understanding and something existing in reality. He uses the painter example: before painting, the painter has the work “in the understanding”; after painting, the work exists both in the understanding and in reality.

This distinction does not amount to a full modern theory of mental representation, but it clearly recognizes intentional inexistence in a broad sense: the mind can have an object before it as conceived, even when the object is not yet or not thereby externally realized. Anselm then adds another distinction: a thing can be “said in the heart” merely by uttering or entertaining the words, or it can be conceived by understanding the thing itself. Thus the fool can say the words “God does not exist,” but cannot, on Anselm’s argument, genuinely understand the object signified as non-existent in the same way.

This is a philosophy-of-mind issue, not merely a premise in an ontological proof. Anselm distinguishes verbal thought, conceptual understanding, and existential commitment. A French study of the Proslogion tradition notes that much debate has centered on whether the inference from “in the understanding” to “in reality” is valid, while less attention has often been given to the exact meaning of esse in solo intellectu and esse in re. That observation is useful: the mental ontology of the Proslogion deserves independent attention.

A reconstructed Anselmian account of intentionality would therefore include at least four levels:

First, there is the level of words or signs, whether spoken outwardly or “said in the heart.” Second, there is the level of understanding, where the mind grasps what the words signify. Third, there is the level of judgment, where the mind takes what-is to be or what-is-not not to be. Fourth, there is the level of reality, where what is conceived may or may not exist independently of the act of conception.

That layered structure is crucial for Anselm’s theory of truth.

5. Truth of thought: mental rightness rather than mere correspondence

In De Veritate, Anselm defines truth as rectitudo, rightness or correctness. The truth of thought consists in thinking that what is, is, and that what is not, is not. A thought is true when it does what thought is for: it judges being and non-being rightly.

This is not simply a correspondence theory in modern dress. A thought’s truth is not only a relation between a mental item and a fact; it is the fulfillment of the thought’s proper norm. A Spanish-language study on Anselm and Aquinas emphasizes exactly this point: Anselm’s rectitudo should not be collapsed into a contemporary truthmaker-style correspondence relation. It has a teleological and normative dimension: a proposition, thought, will, or action is true when it fulfills what it is supposed to do.

Anselm’s definition of truth as rightness “perceptible by mind alone” is especially important. Visible rightness, such as the straightness of a physical object, may be perceived by the senses; the rightness that constitutes truth is grasped only intellectually. This makes truth a specifically mental object, not because truth is subjective, but because its proper mode of apprehension is intellectual.

Anselm also insists that all finite truths depend on Supreme Truth. The truth of things, thoughts, statements, wills, and actions is not a set of disconnected correctnesses. All derive from, or accord with, one Supreme Truth. In De Veritate, he says that Supreme Truth is the cause of other truths or rightnesses, and that truth is one in all true things insofar as those things accord with the Supreme Truth.

For philosophy of mind, this gives us a robustly realist and normative account of cognition. Mental acts are answerable to truth, but truth is not manufactured by the mind. The mind perceives truth intellectually, participates in it, and is corrected by it.

6. Self-consciousness and the internal word

The Monologion contains a subtle theory of self-knowledge. Anselm distinguishes remembering oneself from actively thinking about oneself. A human mind does not always think of itself, yet it remembers itself; when it does think of itself, a kind of internal word is born from memory. By analogy, the supreme Wisdom eternally remembers and thinks itself, but in God there is no temporal alternation between latent memory and actual thought.

This distinction has several consequences. First, self-knowledge is not reducible to episodic introspection. The mind has an abiding self-presence or self-memory even when it is not explicitly attending to itself. Second, actual self-thinking produces an internal likeness or word of the self. Third, the human case is temporal and intermittent, while the divine case is eternal and complete.

Maria Leonor Xavier’s study of Anselm’s theory of knowledge develops this point in a useful way. She stresses that for Anselm the intellect exists in the soul, not in a physical location, and that the intellect’s fundamental competencies are judgment and intuition. She also reads Anselm as distinguishing self-consciousness from consciousness of external things: external things are mediated through sensory images and memory, whereas the thought of oneself is inseparable from the mind that thinks itself.

This does not make Anselm a Cartesian. He does not ground all knowledge on an indubitable ego. But he does treat the rational mind as a privileged mirror: the mind’s ability to remember, understand, and love itself is the closest created analogue to divine self-knowledge.

7. Mental language, imagination, and impossible thought

Anselm’s account of inner speech is broader than the Proslogion alone. In his discussions of divine ineffability and human thought, he repeatedly recognizes that the mind thinks through likenesses, images, and internal words. The Monologion warns that our terms for God are drawn from creatures and therefore cannot express the divine nature properly; we speak by likeness and image.

Xavier’s study highlights that Anselmian cogitatio includes images, impressions of self, intelligible and unintelligible contents, true and false contents, and even simulated non-existence. The limit is contradiction. Thought can range over what is absent, possible, impossible in some respect, or falsely supposed; but when thought collapses into contradiction, it loses genuine intelligibility.

That helps clarify the Proslogion’s treatment of the fool. The fool can entertain words; he can also form a confused or merely verbal thought. But Anselm thinks there are constraints on what can be genuinely understood. Meaningful thought is not just private mental occurrence. It is governed by intelligibility, non-contradiction, and the natures of things.

In modern terms, Anselm offers neither a purely imagistic theory nor a purely linguistic theory of thought. He has something more layered: sensory images, memory, internal words, conceptual understanding, judgment, and intellectual perception of rightness.

8. The will: power, inclination, and act

Anselm’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his theory of will. Contemporary scholarship often notes that Anselm uses “will” in multiple senses. W. Matthews Grant, for example, distinguishes three Anselmian senses: will as the instrument or power in the soul; will as an affection, inclination, or disposition; and will as the actual use or employment of that power in conscious willing. Grant also notes that an affection can remain even when it is not being actively used, as when a person is asleep.

This threefold distinction matters because it prevents crude readings of Anselm. A person may possess the power of will even when not currently willing. A person may have an inclination that is not presently being acted upon. And an actual volition may have a complex intentional structure: one may will one thing for the sake of another thing.

Anselm’s own example in De Libertate Arbitrii is telling. Someone who lies to save his life wills the lie, but not for its own sake; he wills the lie for the sake of life. This reveals the “for-the-sake-of” structure of volition. Mental action is not exhausted by object selection; it includes the end under which the object is willed.

This is philosophically significant. Anselm anticipates a distinction between proximate object and ultimate practical reason. The same outward act, or even the same immediate chosen object, can be morally and psychologically different depending on the end for which it is willed.

9. Freedom: not the ability to sin, but the power to preserve rectitude

Anselm’s definition of free choice is one of the most important elements in his philosophy of mind. In De Libertate Arbitrii, he rejects the idea that freedom is the ability to sin. God and the good angels cannot sin, yet they are free. Indeed, the inability to sin is not a defect of freedom but a higher form of freedom. The ability to sin is a weakness, not an essential component of liberty.

Anselm’s formal definition is that freedom of choice is the ability to preserve uprightness of will for the sake of that uprightness itself. Justice is uprightness of will kept for its own sake; freedom is the rational power by which such uprightness can be preserved.

This definition should be read with care. Anselm is not saying that free choice is merely moral success. The power remains a power of rational nature even when it is not well exercised. He uses analogies such as sight: a person may retain sight even when there is no light or no visible object available. Similarly, freedom belongs to rational nature as long as reason and will remain, even when the person lacks the uprightness that freedom is meant to preserve.

Freedom therefore has three aspects.

First, it is rational: one must have reason by which to recognize uprightness. Second, it is volitional: one must have will by which to hold fast to uprightness. Third, it is normative: its proper end is not random choice, preference satisfaction, or bare self-expression, but the preservation of justice for its own sake.

This is one of Anselm’s most distinctive contributions to philosophy of mind. The will is not free because it floats indeterminately among alternatives. It is free because it can adhere to the right for the sake of the right.

10. The two-affection structure: happiness and justice

The deepest part of Anselm’s moral psychology appears in De Casu Diaboli, his work on the fall of the devil. This treatise should not be treated as an eccentric theological appendix. It is one of Anselm’s most abstract investigations of rational motivation.

Anselm distinguishes two fundamental orientations in rational willing. One is the will for benefit, advantage, or happiness. Every sentient creature seeks benefit and avoids disadvantage; every rational creature also naturally wills happiness. But a rational agent also needs a will for justice or uprightness. If the rational creature had only the will for happiness, it would pursue its own benefit by necessity and would not be just. If it had only the will for justice, there would be no meaningful temptation or meritorious restraint.

The will for justice tempers the will for happiness. A rational creature becomes morally admirable when it refuses to pursue happiness in a way that violates uprightness. This is the background to the angelic fall. The good angels preferred justice and thereby received secure happiness; the bad angels preferred something God had not yet willed to give them, abandoning uprightness through an immoderate will for benefit.

This gives Anselm a sophisticated theory of motivation. Human and angelic minds are not moved by cognition alone, nor by desire alone. They are structured by competing rational affections: the drive toward happiness and the love of justice. A healthy mind does not abolish the desire for happiness; it orders that desire by justice.

Modern scholarship continues to debate the rationality of the angelic fall. One recent Cambridge article frames the problem as both a “hard problem” and a “harder problem”: the fall must be free and morally significant, but also in some sense subjectively rational to the agent. That contemporary debate confirms that Anselm’s angelology is not a mere curiosity; it is a rigorous test case for theories of agency, preference, and irrational choice.

11. Angelic minds as philosophically necessary cases

Anselm’s angels are finite rational minds without human embodiment. Their importance lies partly in that abstraction. They allow Anselm to ask: what belongs to rational agency as such, apart from bodily appetite, social conditioning, and sensory limitation?

The good angels show that freedom is compatible with perfected inability to sin. In Anselm’s framework, their post-confirmation inability to sin is not mechanical determinism; it is the stability of a will that has preserved justice. The fallen angels show the opposite: a rational will can abandon uprightness by willing its own advantage immoderately.

This angelic material clarifies at least five features of Anselmian mind.

First, rational mind is not essentially embodied, even though human rational mind is embodied. Second, freedom does not require the permanent availability of evil alternatives. Third, self-interest is not evil in itself, but it becomes disordered when it refuses justice. Fourth, sin is not a positive substance in the will but a privation of right order. Fifth, once justice is lost, the fallen rational creature cannot restore it by its own unaided power.

The angelic mind is therefore not peripheral. It is Anselm’s cleanest philosophical model of finite rational agency.

12. Evil in the mind: privation, not positive substance

Anselm’s account of evil is inseparable from his theory of mind. A bad will is not bad because willing as such is an evil entity. The will and the turning of the will are real beings, and as beings they are good. Evil consists in the absence of justice where justice ought to be. Injustice is a privation of uprightness.

This has a precise psychological implication. Moral evil is not a dark substance lodged inside the soul. Nor is it merely a mistaken belief. It is a deprivation in rational-volitional order: the will lacks the rectitude it ought to preserve. The bad mind is disordered not because it has too much being, but because something due to it is missing.

This privation theory also protects divine causality from a crude implication. God creates and sustains what is real in creatures and their acts, but the defect or absence by which an act is evil is not a positive entity God creates. Grant’s discussion of Anselm’s account of sinful action emphasizes that Anselm attributes what is real in creaturely acts to divine causality while denying that the lack or privation is caused in the same way.

Anselm is not thereby solving every modern problem about evil, agency, and divine causation. But he is giving a disciplined metaphysical account: evil in the mind is parasitic on good powers. The very powers by which one sins—intellect, will, desire, agency—are good as powers; the evil is their privative disorder.

13. Divine mind: Wisdom, Word, memory, love, and simplicity

The mind of God is not an optional theological add-on to Anselm’s philosophy of mind. It is the source and standard of every finite mind. But Anselm does not simply project human psychology onto God. He uses human mind analogically and then corrects the analogy by divine simplicity, eternity, and ineffability.

In the Monologion, Anselm presents the Son as the true Word or perfect intelligence of the Father. The supreme Wisdom remembers itself, conceives itself, and loves itself. In the human mind, memory, understanding, and love are distinguishable and temporally variable; in God, they are not separable faculties or episodes. God’s memory, intelligence, and love are identical with the divine essence.

The Proslogion reinforces this through divine simplicity. God’s life, wisdom, goodness, and other attributes are not parts added together. They are one whole. God is not a composite mind containing separate modules named intellect, will, memory, and affection.

This makes divine mind both model and limit. God is the model because finite minds image divine memory, intelligence, and love. God is the limit because finite minds cannot comprehend divine unity directly. Anselm repeatedly emphasizes that our terms for God are inadequate, drawn from creatures, and used by likeness.

A careful Anselmian philosophy of mind must therefore say: human mental structure is genuinely revelatory of divine mind, but only analogically. The human mind is a mirror, not a duplicate.

14. Divine cognition: non-sensory, non-discursive, atemporal

Anselm’s God does not know through bodily sense. In the Proslogion, Anselm considers whether God is “sensible” and answers analogically: if sensing is cognition, or ordered toward cognition, then God may be said to sense because God cognizes all things supremely; but God does not do this through corporeal sense organs.

This implies a hierarchy of cognition. Human beings often know through bodily senses, images, and discursive reasoning. God knows immediately, perfectly, and without bodily mediation. Divine cognition is not less than sensory knowledge; it is beyond it.

Anselm’s treatment of divine eternity in De Concordia extends the point. God’s “foreknowledge” is not foreknowledge in the ordinary temporal sense. In eternity there is no before and after; all times are present to God. Anselm explicitly says that terms such as foreknowledge and predestination are not properly applied to God in a temporal way, because all things are present at once to divine eternity.

This yields a profound contrast between finite and divine mind. Human cognition is temporal, partial, sequential, and often inferential. Divine cognition is eternal, total, and non-discursive. God does not wait, remember in the human sense, predict, update, or infer. God knows in an eternal present.

15. Divine affect: compassion without passive passion

Anselm’s account of divine compassion is equally important. In the Proslogion, he asks how God can be compassionate if God is impassible. His answer is that God is compassionate according to our experience of God’s effects, but not by being passively affected in himself. We experience God’s mercy; God does not undergo a passion the way creatures do.

This should not be dismissed as cold or anti-affective. Rather, Anselm is distinguishing created affective passivity from divine active goodness. Human emotions often involve vulnerability, reception, alteration, and disturbance. God’s mercy is not less real because it lacks passivity. It is more perfect because it is not dependent on being moved from outside.

For philosophy of mind, this introduces a distinction between affect as passive undergoing and love as active perfection. Created minds love through change, need, attention, and susceptibility. Divine love is not a passion but the eternal identity of God’s goodness, will, and love.

16. Divine will, justice, and freedom

Anselm’s account of divine freedom follows directly from his account of human freedom. If freedom were the ability to sin, God would not be free. Since that conclusion is absurd for Anselm, the definition must be different. True freedom is the power to preserve uprightness of will for its own sake; God has this supremely because God’s will and uprightness are not distinct. In De Veritate, Anselm says that in God will and uprightness are not separate, and that God’s uprightness keeps itself by itself and for itself.

Divine freedom is therefore not arbitrary choice among possibilities. It is perfect, self-identical rectitude. God’s will is free because it is not compelled by anything external and cannot fall away from the good. Again, the good angels are closer to this condition than fallen human beings are: their inability to sin is a perfected stability, not a loss of freedom.

This is one of Anselm’s most counter-modern claims. Freedom does not reach its height in open-ended alternative possibility. It reaches its height in indefectible justice.

17. Grace and the wounded human mind

Anselm’s account of the human mind is not optimistic in a Pelagian sense. The rational creature has reason and will; freedom remains as a natural capacity of rational nature. But the fallen mind cannot simply generate justice from itself after losing it. In De Casu Diaboli, Anselm says that the renegade angel cannot return to justice by himself; before and after receiving justice, he does not have justice from himself.

De Concordia develops the relation between grace and free choice. Anselm explicitly tries to reconcile divine foreknowledge, predestination, grace, and free choice. He argues that free choice and grace coexist and cooperate: no one receives or keeps uprightness except through grace, yet scriptural emphasis on grace does not abolish free choice, and scriptural emphasis on free choice does not abolish grace. Grace makes free choice effective in preserving or recovering uprightness.

This gives Anselm a participatory theory of agency. The finite mind is real, responsible, and active, but not self-sufficient. It depends on divine truth for the norms of cognition and on divine grace for the restoration and preservation of justice.

In contemporary terms, Anselm rejects both reduction and autonomy. The mind is not reducible to impersonal mechanism; but neither is it an isolated self-legislating subject. It is a created rational power whose fulfillment depends on participation in truth, justice, and grace.

18. Non-English and non-Anglophone interpretive perspectives

Several non-Anglophone or non-Anglo-centered discussions help bring out aspects of Anselm’s philosophy of mind that can be flattened in English-language treatments focused almost entirely on the ontological argument.

A Spanish article by Patricia Moya Cañas emphasizes that Anselm’s rectitudo is perceptible only by reason and should not be reduced to modern correspondence. It is teleological and normative: statements, thoughts, actions, and wills are true when they fulfill the purpose or debere proper to them. This supports reading Anselm’s theory of mind as fundamentally norm-governed rather than merely representational.

An Italian study by Paola Anna Maria Müller presents De Veritate as a systematic hinge in which truth unfolds from linguistic and logical truth into ontological and moral rightness. The same study emphasizes rectitudo as conformity of being, thinking, and willing to their proper debere, while warning against a simple assimilation of Anselm to modern Kantian autonomy, since Anselm’s freedom remains grounded in God.

Another Italian discussion in Acta Philosophica stresses the centrality of justice, defining justice as uprightness of will preserved for its own sake and freedom as the power to preserve that uprightness. It also reads Anselm as resisting a merely psychologistic inwardness: introspection is not for ego-enclosure, but for seeing God, truth, and justice through the rational mind.

The French discussion of esse in solo intellectu and esse in re is useful because it shifts attention from the validity of the ontological proof alone to the meaning of being in the understanding. That shift is essential for recovering Anselm’s implicit theory of intentionality and conceptual existence.

A German study by Christian Ströbele treats rectitudo as a formal horizon joining truth and justice, theoretical and practical reason, freedom and Geistigkeit. This is close to the interpretation advanced here: Anselm’s mind is not divided into an epistemic faculty on one side and an ethical faculty on the other. Truth and justice are structurally linked by rightness.

Finally, Xavier’s study, published in the Portuguese journal Mediaevalia, is valuable for reconstructing Anselm’s theory of knowledge. It highlights the role of judgment, intuition, self-consciousness, memory, internal speech, and the mind as mirror. That makes it especially important for any attempt to extract a comprehensive philosophy of mind rather than merely a doctrine of free will or a theory of the ontological argument.

The non-English reception therefore confirms a broad conclusion: Anselm’s key concept for philosophy of mind is not “consciousness” in the modern narrow sense, but rectitudo as the norm binding being, thinking, willing, and loving.

19. Systematic reconstruction: Anselm’s philosophy of mind in theses

A comprehensive Anselmian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed in the following theses.

Thesis 1: Mind is a created rational power ordered toward God

Human mind is not self-originating. It is part of the rational soul, created in the divine image so that it may remember, understand, and love God. Its highest object is not itself, the world, or happiness considered in isolation, but the supreme Being.

Thesis 2: Mind images God through memory, intelligence, and love

The rational mind is the best created mirror of God because it can remember, understand, and love itself and God. This triadic structure reflects the divine triad of memory, Word/intelligence, and love, though only analogically and imperfectly in creatures.

Thesis 3: Mental acts are intentional and layered

The mind can have things “in the understanding” before or apart from their existence in reality. It can entertain words, form concepts, judge propositions, and grasp or fail to grasp objects. The Proslogion’s distinction between verbal saying and genuine understanding is a key element of Anselmian intentionality.

Thesis 4: Thought is governed by truth as rightness

Truth is not merely subjective coherence or psychological conviction. A thought is true when it rightly thinks what is as being and what is not as not being. Truth is rectitudo perceptible by mind alone and ultimately dependent on Supreme Truth.

Thesis 5: Rationality is practical and affective

A rational creature can distinguish true from false, just from unjust, good from bad, and advantageous from disadvantageous. But this discernment is incomplete unless the creature loves or rejects accordingly. Cognition is ordered toward love and action.

Thesis 6: Will has power, dispositional, and actual dimensions

The will is not only a momentary choice. It is a power of the soul, a structure of inclinations or affections, and an actual use in willing. This allows Anselm to analyze latent motivation, actual choice, and the deeper architecture of rational agency.

Thesis 7: Free choice is for justice, not for sin

Freedom is the ability to preserve uprightness of will for the sake of that uprightness. The power to sin is not the essence of freedom; it is a defect or vulnerability. God and the good angels are freer, not less free, because they cannot sin.

Thesis 8: Rational motivation has two basic orientations

The rational will seeks happiness or benefit, but it must also love justice. Justice regulates the pursuit of happiness. Without the will for justice, the mind collapses into self-interested necessity; without the will for happiness, there is no meaningful test of justice.

Thesis 9: Evil in the mind is privative disorder

A bad will is not evil as a positive being. The act, power, and turning of the will are good insofar as they exist. Evil is the absence of justice where justice ought to be.

Thesis 10: Divine mind is the simple, eternal source of finite mentality

God’s mind is not a magnified human psychology. God’s wisdom, memory, intelligence, love, will, justice, and truth are identical with the divine essence. God knows without bodily sense, without discursive inference, and without temporal succession.

Thesis 11: Angelic minds are indispensable philosophical cases

Angels reveal finite rational agency without embodiment. The good angels show perfected freedom; the fallen angels show how rational will can abandon justice through immoderate desire for benefit. Angelic psychology is therefore central to Anselm’s theory of mind.

Thesis 12: The fallen mind needs grace without losing agency

Free choice remains a real power of rational nature, but fallen rational creatures cannot restore justice by unaided self-generation. Grace and free choice cooperate: grace gives and sustains uprightness, while free choice preserves it when enabled.

20. Comparison with modern categories

Anselm’s philosophy of mind resists easy classification.

It is not Cartesian dualism in the strict modern sense. Anselm treats the rational soul as intellectual, self-reflexive, and not reducible to bodily sense, but he does not ground philosophy on the isolated certainty of the ego. Self-knowledge is important because the mind is a mirror of God, not because the self is the final foundation of metaphysics.

It is not reductive naturalism. Mental acts are governed by truth, justice, and rightness, and those norms are not reducible to causal processes or subjective preferences. For Anselm, normativity is metaphysically basic because it is grounded in Supreme Truth.

It is not voluntarism in the sense that the will creates value by choosing. The will is free when it preserves rightness, not when it arbitrarily posits ends. Divine will is not arbitrary either, because in God will and uprightness are identical.

It is not simple intellectualism. The mind does not reach fulfillment by knowing alone. Memory and understanding must become love. Rational discernment is useless unless the mind loves or rejects according to what it discerns.

It is not simple eudaimonism. Happiness is a genuine object of rational desire, but the pursuit of happiness must be moderated by justice. The fallen angel’s error is not desiring happiness, but willing benefit in a way not governed by uprightness.

It is not a standard libertarian theory of free will if libertarianism is defined by alternative possibilities or the ability to do otherwise in the sense of being able to sin. Anselm’s highest cases of freedom—God and the good angels—lack the ability to sin. For him, perfected freedom is stable adherence to justice.

21. Strengths of Anselm’s account

Anselm’s greatest strength is integration. He does not split cognition, volition, affect, normativity, and metaphysics into separate disciplines. A mind thinks truly, wills rightly, loves properly, and acts freely only when these dimensions are ordered together.

A second strength is his account of normativity. By defining truth, justice, and freedom through rectitudo, he gives a unified account of correctness in thought, will, and action. This is philosophically powerful because it avoids treating truth as merely theoretical and justice as merely practical.

A third strength is his moral psychology of motivation. The distinction between the will for happiness and the will for justice allows him to explain why self-interest is natural but insufficient, why temptation is possible, and why moral worth requires more than preference satisfaction.

A fourth strength is his treatment of freedom as excellence rather than indifference. The claim that God and the good angels are free precisely because they cannot sin challenges modern assumptions, but it also gives Anselm a way to connect liberty with moral perfection rather than with instability.

A fifth strength is his use of divine and angelic minds as philosophical boundary cases. God tests what mind would be without finitude, discursiveness, passivity, or temporal succession. Angels test what finite rational agency would be without human embodiment. Human beings then occupy a middle position: embodied, temporal, self-reflexive, free, wounded, and grace-dependent.

22. Difficulties and open questions

Anselm gives little by way of a detailed mind-body theory. He recognizes bodily sense, images, and the difference between corporeal and intellectual cognition, but he does not offer anything like a systematic account of embodiment, perception, brain function, or psychosomatic integration. This is not a failure by his own standards, but it is a limitation for modern philosophy of mind.

His account of animal cognition is also underdeveloped. He allows that sentient beings seek benefit and avoid disadvantage, but rationality is marked by the ability to discern justice, truth, and value in a higher sense. A contemporary reader may want a more gradual account of non-human cognition and affect.

The first sin remains a hard problem. If the angelic mind was rational and originally upright, why did it abandon justice? Anselm’s two-affection structure explains how temptation is possible, but interpreters continue to debate whether it fully explains the subjective rationality of the first evil choice.

Divine simplicity also creates pressure. If God’s knowing, willing, loving, justice, and essence are identical, then divine mind is radically unlike finite mind. Anselm accepts this and relies on analogy, but the exact relation between human psychological analogy and divine simplicity remains philosophically delicate.

Finally, grace and free choice remain in tension. Anselm wants both real creaturely agency and dependence on grace. De Concordia argues that grace and free choice cooperate, but the precise metaphysical mechanics remain difficult, especially for readers seeking a fully explicit causal model.

23. Final synthesis

Anselm’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence: mind is a rational, self-reflexive, truth-perceiving, love-bearing power whose freedom consists in preserving the right for its own sake, and whose fulfillment lies in remembering, understanding, and loving God.

Human mind is an image: finite, temporal, often darkened, but capable of self-memory, understanding, and love. Angelic mind is a finite rational case purified of embodiment, showing both perfected freedom and the possibility of catastrophic privation. Divine mind is not one mind among others, but the simple, eternal, uncreated source of truth, wisdom, justice, will, and love.

The deepest Anselmian category is therefore not “consciousness” but rightness. A mind is not explained adequately by asking only what it experiences, represents, or chooses. One must also ask whether it thinks truly, wills justly, loves rightly, and preserves uprightness for its own sake. That is the core of Anselm’s philosophy of mind.

PreviousGersonides (Ralbag) NextBonaventure

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
